perturbation.original_text: You will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either we or our financial advisor, Mesirow Financial, Inc. (“Mesirow”), or our other representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined hereinafter) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the “Evaluation Materials”). You recognize and acknowledge the competitive value of the Evaluation Materials and the damage that could result to the Company if the Evaluation Materials were used or disclosed except as authorized by this agreement
perturbation.changed_text: You will endeavor to treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either we or our financial advisor, Mesirow Financial, Inc. (“Mesirow”), or our other representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined hereinafter) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the “Evaluation Materials”). You acknowledge the competitive value of the Evaluation Materials, but liability for damages resulting from unauthorized use or disclosure will be evaluated reasonably.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will treat confidentially' with 'will endeavor to treat confidentially' weakens the obligation. Adding 'liability for damages resulting from unauthorized use or disclosure will be evaluated reasonably' introduces ambiguity regarding the standards for assessing and enforcing liability, making it less strict than the original agreement. This introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA provides specific legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, including injunctive relief and damages. The modification, by using 'endeavor' and introducing the concept of 'evaluated reasonably', makes the obligation to protect confidential information less stringent and the assessment of damages more discretionary. This could undermine the protections afforded by CUTSA, as it allows for a weaker standard of care in protecting trade secrets and a more subjective determination of damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text states "will treat confidentially," establishing a clear obligation. The changed text replaces this with "will endeavor to treat confidentially," which weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. Furthermore, the addition of "liability for damages resulting from unauthorized use or disclosure will be evaluated reasonably" makes the determination of liability subjective, potentially undermining the legal remedies provided by trade secret laws. The law snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines "Improper means" which includes "breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy". By weakening the duty to maintain secrecy, the changed text creates ambiguity about the standard of care required, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation under CUTSA, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You and your representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) such information may only be disclosed to those of your directors, officers, employees, affiliates, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), and lenders (collectively, “your representatives”) who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company (it being understood that your representatives shall be informed by you of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by you, and shall each expressly agree (in writing, if requested by the Company or its advisors), to treat such information confidential in accordance with the agreement) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: You and your representatives will strive to keep the Evaluation Materials confidential; provided, however, that (i) such information may be disclosed to those of your directors, officers, employees, affiliates, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), and lenders (collectively, “your representatives”) who have a potential need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company (it being preferred that your representatives are informed by you of the confidential nature of such information and are directed by you to treat such information confidential in accordance with the agreement) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may be made if the Company is generally aware of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will keep completely confidential' to 'will strive to keep confidential' weakens the obligation. Changing 'need to know' to 'have a potential need to know' broadens the scope of permissible disclosure. Replacing 'shall be informed' with 'it being preferred that' makes informing representatives of confidentiality optional. Finally, changing 'Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure' to 'Company is generally aware of such disclosure' reduces the requirement for explicit written consent. All of these changes weaken the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA imposes strict requirements for businesses regarding the handling of consumer data. The original text ensured tight control over confidential information, including restrictions on disclosure and a requirement for written consent for any exceptions. By weakening these controls, the modified text makes it easier for Evaluation Materials (which could include consumer data) to be disclosed without proper safeguards, potentially violating the CCPA's provisions regarding data security and consumer privacy rights. The change also conflicts with the CCPA's requirement for explicit consent before sharing consumer data with third parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.100 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original contract's confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with data protection principles outlined in the CCPA. The original text mandated keeping Evaluation Materials 'completely confidential,' while the revised version states the party 'will strive to keep' the materials confidential, which weakens the original obligation. Furthermore, the original contract required that representatives 'need to know' the information, but this was weakened in the updated text to 'have a potential need to know' which potentially broadens the scope of information disclosure, and therefore increases the risk of violating the CCPA. Snippet 2 states the CCPA gives consumers more control over their personal information that businesses collect, and that businesses have responsibilities to respond to consumer requests and explain their privacy practices. By weakening the original confidentiality obligations, the modified text potentially undermines the consumer's control over their personal information.

perturbation.original_text: In any event, neither you nor any of your representatives will use the Evaluation Materials in any way which is or may be detrimental to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, neither you nor any of your representatives will knowingly use the Evaluation Materials in any way that causes material and direct detrimental harm to the Company's ongoing operations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause prohibits *any* detrimental use. The modified clause introduces several ambiguities and limitations: (1) 'Knowingly' requires proof of intent. (2) 'Material and direct detrimental harm' requires a higher threshold of harm and direct causation. (3) 'Ongoing operations' limits the scope of protection to a specific aspect of the company's business. These changes weaken the protection and make it more difficult for the Company to prove a violation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 (Unfair Competition Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL) prohibits any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice. The original clause in the contract broadly prohibited any detrimental use of Evaluation Materials, which aligns with the UCL's goal of preventing unfair competition. By narrowing the prohibition to 'knowingly' causing 'material and direct detrimental harm to the Company's ongoing operations,' the modified clause creates a loophole that could allow for uses of the information that are unfair or unethical but do not meet the stringent criteria for a violation. This weakened protection could allow a party to engage in activities that, while not directly and materially harming ongoing operations, still provide an unfair competitive advantage derived from the confidential Evaluation Materials, thus undermining the UCL.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified clause introduces ambiguity that weakens the company's protection against unfair competition, potentially contradicting California Business and Professions Code Section 17200. The original text broadly prohibited 'any' detrimental use, whereas the changed text limits the prohibition to 'knowingly' causing 'material and direct detrimental harm to the Company's ongoing operations.' This introduces intent and causation requirements that the original text avoids. Section 17200 defines unfair competition as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice.' The modified clause could allow a party to engage in uses of the Evaluation Materials that are unfair but don't meet the higher threshold established, potentially undermining the UCL's intent to prevent unfair business practices.

perturbation.original_text: You further understand and agree that (i) the Company and Mesirow shall be free to conduct the process for the Company’s sale as they in their sole discretion shall determine (including, without limitation, negotiating with any of the prospective buyers and entering into a Sale Agreement without prior notice to you or to any other person), (ii) any procedures relating to such sale may be changed at any time without notice to you or any other person and (iii) you shall not have any claims whatsoever against the Company, Mesirow or any of their respective directors, officers, employees, stockholders, owners, affiliates, agents or representatives arising out of or relating to the sale of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: You further understand and agree that (i) the Company and Mesirow will generally conduct the process for the Company’s sale as they reasonably determine (including, without limitation, negotiating with any of the prospective buyers and entering into a Sale Agreement without undue delay, unless otherwise impractical), (ii) any procedures relating to such sale may be adjusted as needed without comprehensive notice to you or any other person and (iii) you will aim to minimize any claims against the Company, Mesirow or any of their respective directors, officers, employees, stockholders, owners, affiliates, agents or representatives arising out of or relating to the sale of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: These modifications introduce ambiguity. 'Shall be free to conduct...as they in their sole discretion shall determine' is replaced with 'will generally conduct...as they reasonably determine', adding a requirement for reasonableness. 'Without prior notice' is weakened to 'without undue delay, unless otherwise impractical'. 'May be changed at any time without notice' is weakened to 'may be adjusted as needed without comprehensive notice'. 'Shall not have any claims whatsoever' is replaced with 'will aim to minimize any claims'. All of these changes weaken the company's protections and introduce uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 10b-5 prohibits fraudulent or deceptive practices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original language provided the Company with broad discretion in conducting the sale process and limited the potential buyer's claims. The weakened language introduces the possibility of misrepresentation or concealment of material facts during the sale process if the 'reasonable determination' is not made in good faith or if 'undue delay' is manipulated. The modified language introduces ambiguity and weakens the company's safeguards, creating a risk that actions taken under the guise of 'reasonable' business decisions could still violate Rule 10b-5 if they deceive or defraud potential investors or the market.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protections afforded by SEC Rule 10b-5, creating a contradiction. The original contract language provided broad discretion to the Company, limiting potential claims. The changed text replaces 'sole discretion' with 'reasonably determine', 'without prior notice' with 'without undue delay, unless otherwise impractical', 'may be changed at any time without notice' with 'may be adjusted as needed without comprehensive notice', and 'shall not have any claims whatsoever' with 'will aim to minimize any claims'. These modifications, as the Law Explanation states, introduce ambiguity and weaken the company's safeguards. This creates a risk that actions taken under the guise of 'reasonable' business decisions could still violate Rule 10b-5 if they deceive or defraud potential investors. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states it is unlawful 'To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' or 'To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' By weakening the original, more protective language, the changed text increases the potential for actions that could be construed as fraudulent or deceptive under Rule 10b-5, thus creating a contradiction.
